title: Signal Dry Bulk Weekly Report
summary:
Main
The third week of November is characterized by a continued weaker trend in freight rates and demand for smaller vessel categories, while there are signs of stronger momentum in Capesize. The potential for strength in the coming days was reflected in iron ore prices. According to Reuters news agency, the spot price of iron ore for delivery to northern China has recovered from a two-year low and is up $92/tonne, but is still 43% below its 2022 peak of $160/tonne reached on March 8 in the wake of geopolitical tensions between Russia and Ukraine.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The third week of November is characterized by a continued weaker trend in freight rates and demand for smaller vessel categories, while there are signs of stronger momentum in Capesize. The potential for strength in the coming days was reflected in iron ore prices.

children.title: SECTION 1/ FREIGHT - Market Rates ($/t) Weaker
children.summary:
‘The Big Picture' - Capesize and Panamax Bulkers and Smaller Ship Sizes
Image reference: unnamed - 2022-11-17T123139.315.png | assets/2022-11-17_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2022-11-17t123139315_948fd23bc404.png
The Capesize segment continues to show an upward trend, while the weaker outlook for freight rates in the other size classes emerged in the last days of November. Capesize freight rates are now approaching $21/tonne, up nearly $2/tonne from two weeks ago, but still $6/tonne lower than week 40.

children.title: SECTION 2/ SUPPLY - Ballasters (# vessels) Increasing
children.summary:
Supply Trend Lines for Key Load Areas
Image reference: unnamed - 2022-11-17T123308.100.png | assets/2022-11-17_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2022-11-17t123308100_b1c2e0405607.png
The number of ships in ballast remains above the annual average in November for the smaller ship categories, while a downward trend is observed in the Capesize and Panamax segments. Capesize SE Africa: The number of vessels is now close to the annual average of 81, 3 more than the previous week, after peaking in week 43.

children.title: SECTION 3/ DEMAND - TonDays Decreasing
children.summary:
Image reference: unnamed - 2022-11-17T123431.592.png | assets/2022-11-17_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2022-11-17t123431592_c671a0a6afdd.png
Demand growth continues to decline with the exception of the Capesize segment, while the small ship categories are facing further weakness. Capesize demand ton-days: The percentage increase remained at the highest level since the last peak in week 27.

children.title: SECTION 4/ PORT CONGESTION - No of Vessels Increasing
section_path: SECTION 4/ PORT CONGESTION - No of Vessels Increasing
children.section_path_text: SECTION 4/ PORT CONGESTION - No of Vessels Increasing
children.summary:
Dry bulk ships congested at Chinese ports
Image reference: unnamed - 2022-11-17T125414.948.png | assets/2022-11-17_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_unnamed-2022-11-17t125414948_68086aeedbf9.png
The new highs in total bulk carrier congestion this week are led by the larger vessel categories, Capesize and Panamax. Capesize: The number of vessels increased to 145, up 24 from the previous week.
